content.text: SBOM Generation for GenAI Application Dependencies

A Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) is a machine-readable inventory of every component in a software artifact: Python packages, OS packages, container base images, and the transitive dependencies of all of the above. For GenAI services, SBOMs are especially valuable because ML library stacks (PyTorch, Transformers, LangChain) bring hundreds of transitive dependencies, many with CVEs that would be invisible without a complete inventory.

Generating an SBOM at build time and attaching it to the container image as a signed attestation means that at any point in the future — including during a security incident — you can answer: exactly what was running in production at image digest X, and was component Y present?

attrs.alt: SBOM generation pipeline: source → scan → SBOM → sign → store

content.text: Generating an SBOM with Syft

Syft (by Anchore) generates CycloneDX and SPDX SBOMs from container images, directories, and lock files. For a GenAI service, run syft against the built container image to capture both the Python packages installed in the venv and the OS-level packages in the base image.

# Install syft
curl -sSfL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anchore/syft/main/install.sh | sh -s -- -b /usr/local/bin

# Generate SBOM from the built container image in SPDX JSON format
syft registry.internal/genai-service:$IMAGE_TAG \
    --output spdx-json=sbom.spdx.json \
    --output cyclonedx-json=sbom.cyclonedx.json

# Also scan from source (catches dev-only deps before containerization)
syft dir:. \
    --output spdx-json=sbom-source.spdx.json \
    --config .syft.yaml

# .syft.yaml — syft configuration for GenAI service
catalog:
  package:
    search-indexed-archives: true
    search-unindexed-archives: false

file:
  metadata:
    digests: ["sha256"]

# Include Python virtual envs (common in ML containers)
scope: AllLayers

exclude:
  - "**/.git/**"
  - "**/node_modules/**"
  - "**/__pycache__/**"
Scanning for Vulnerabilities with Grype

An SBOM without vulnerability matching is just an inventory list. Grype (also by Anchore) takes an SBOM file and matches every component against the NVD, GitHub Advisories, and OS vendor databases to report CVEs.

# Install grype
curl -sSfL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anchore/grype/main/install.sh | sh -s -- -b /usr/local/bin

# Scan the SBOM for vulnerabilities
grype sbom:sbom.spdx.json --output table

# Fail CI if any HIGH or CRITICAL CVEs found without an exception
grype sbom:sbom.spdx.json \
    --fail-on high \
    --config .grype.yaml \
    --output json > grype-report.json

# .grype.yaml — vulnerability gate configuration
ignore:
  # Example: known false positive in numpy with no fix available
  - vulnerability: CVE-2023-XXXXX
    package:
      name: numpy
      version: "1.26.4"
    reason: "No fix available upstream; mitigated by network isolation"

fail:
  - severity: critical
  - severity: high

output:
  format: json
  file: grype-report.json
Maintain an ignore list for accepted CVEs with documented rationale. An undocumented ignore is indistinguishable from a forgotten one — require reason and expiry fields.
Signing the SBOM as an OCI Attestation

Attaching the SBOM to the container image as a signed attestation links the inventory to a specific immutable image digest. Anyone pulling the image can verify the SBOM was produced by your CI pipeline and has not been tampered with.

# Sign the SBOM and attach it to the image using cosign (keyless via OIDC)
# Requires: COSIGN_EXPERIMENTAL=1, running in GitHub Actions with id-token: write

cosign attest \
    --predicate sbom.spdx.json \
    --type spdxjson \
    registry.internal/genai-service@sha256:$IMAGE_DIGEST

# Verify the attestation (run at deploy time or by consumers)
cosign verify-attestation \
    --type spdxjson \
    --certificate-identity-regexp "https://github.com/org/genai-service/.*" \
    --certificate-oidc-issuer https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com \
    registry.internal/genai-service@sha256:$IMAGE_DIGEST \
    | jq '.payload | @base64d | fromjson | .predicate.packages | length'

# .github/workflows/build.yml — SBOM generation and attestation step
- name: Generate SBOM
  run: |
    syft ${{ env.IMAGE_REF }} --output spdx-json=sbom.spdx.json
    grype sbom:sbom.spdx.json --fail-on high --config .grype.yaml

- name: Attest SBOM to image
  env:
    COSIGN_EXPERIMENTAL: "1"
  run: |
    cosign attest \
      --predicate sbom.spdx.json \
      --type spdxjson \
      ${{ env.IMAGE_REF }}@${{ steps.build.outputs.digest }}

- name: Upload SBOM to S3 archive
  run: |
    aws s3 cp sbom.spdx.json \
      s3://genai-sbom-archive/${{ github.sha }}/sbom.spdx.json
    aws s3 cp grype-report.json \
      s3://genai-sbom-archive/${{ github.sha }}/grype-report.json
Tracking Model Versions Alongside Dependencies

Standard SBOM formats (SPDX, CycloneDX) have no built-in concept of an LLM model accessed via API. Extend your SBOM metadata with a companion model-registry entry that records which model versions the service called at each build.

# scripts/record_model_versions.py
# Writes a model-manifest.json alongside the SBOM for regulatory and audit use.

import json, datetime, hashlib, pathlib

MODEL_MANIFEST = {
    "generated_at": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
    "service": "genai-service",
    "models": [
        {
            "provider": "openai",
            "model_id": "gpt-4o-2024-05-13",
            "access_type": "api",
            "purpose": "primary_inference",
            "max_tokens": 4096,
        },
        {
            "provider": "anthropic",
            "model_id": "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
            "access_type": "api",
            "purpose": "fallback_inference",
            "max_tokens": 8192,
        },
        {
            "provider": "openai",
            "model_id": "text-embedding-3-small",
            "access_type": "api",
            "purpose": "embedding",
            "dimensions": 1536,
        },
    ],
}

# Compute a deterministic fingerprint for the model set
fingerprint_input = json.dumps(MODEL_MANIFEST["models"], sort_keys=True)
MODEL_MANIFEST["models_sha256"] = hashlib.sha256(
    fingerprint_input.encode()
).hexdigest()

out_path = pathlib.Path("model-manifest.json")
out_path.write_text(json.dumps(MODEL_MANIFEST, indent=2))
print(f"Model manifest written: {out_path} ({len(MODEL_MANIFEST['models'])} models)")

Commit model-manifest.json to the build artifacts alongside the SBOM. When a new model version is deployed (e.g., migrating from gpt-4o-2024-05-13 to gpt-4o-2024-08-06), the manifest diff in the PR makes the model version bump visible in code review.
SBOM Formats at a Glance
Format
Version
Use Case
Tool Support
SPDX JSON
Compliance, government, broad tooling
syft, trivy, many SCA tools
CycloneDX JSON
Security-focused, richer vuln metadata
syft, trivy, Dependency-Track
CycloneDX XML
Enterprise integrations (JIRA, ServiceNow)
Dependency-Track export
SPDX Tag-Value
Lightweight text format, easy to diff
syft, SPDX tools

Generate both SPDX and CycloneDX at build time — the cost is negligible (syft runs in under 30s for most GenAI images) and different consumers expect different formats. Store both in the S3 archive under the same commit SHA.
Dependency-Track for Continuous SBOM Monitoring

Generating an SBOM at build time is only the first step. New CVEs are published daily against packages that were clean at build time. Dependency-Track (OWASP) ingests your SBOMs and monitors them continuously against the NVD — alerting you when a vulnerability is disclosed for a component already running in production.

# Upload SBOM to Dependency-Track after each build
# Requires: DT_API_KEY and DT_BASE_URL set as CI secrets

DT_PROJECT_NAME="genai-service"
DT_PROJECT_VERSION="${GITHUB_SHA:0:8}"

# Upload CycloneDX format (preferred by Dependency-Track)
curl -X PUT \
    -H "X-Api-Key: $DT_API_KEY" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d "{
      \"projectName\": \"$DT_PROJECT_NAME\",
      \"projectVersion\": \"$DT_PROJECT_VERSION\",
      \"autoCreate\": true,
      \"bom\": \"$(base64 -w0 sbom.cyclonedx.json)\"
    }" \
    "$DT_BASE_URL/api/v1/bom"

echo "SBOM uploaded to Dependency-Track for project $DT_PROJECT_NAME@$DT_PROJECT_VERSION"

# scripts/check_dt_findings.py
# Polls Dependency-Track API for HIGH/CRITICAL findings on the latest production build.
# Run nightly to catch CVEs published after the last build.

import requests, os, sys, json

DT_BASE = os.environ["DT_BASE_URL"]
DT_KEY = os.environ["DT_API_KEY"]
PROJECT_NAME = "genai-service"
SEVERITY_BLOCK = {"CRITICAL", "HIGH"}

def get_project_uuid(name: str) -> str:
    resp = requests.get(f"{DT_BASE}/api/v1/project",
                        params={"name": name},
                        headers={"X-Api-Key": DT_KEY})
    resp.raise_for_status()
    projects = resp.json()
    if not projects:
        raise ValueError(f"Project '{name}' not found in Dependency-Track")
    return projects[0]["uuid"]

def get_findings(project_uuid: str) -> list[dict]:
    resp = requests.get(f"{DT_BASE}/api/v1/finding/project/{project_uuid}",
                        headers={"X-Api-Key": DT_KEY})
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    uuid = get_project_uuid(PROJECT_NAME)
    findings = get_findings(uuid)
    blocking = [f for f in findings if f["vulnerability"]["severity"] in SEVERITY_BLOCK]

    print(f"Total findings: {len(findings)}, blocking: {len(blocking)}")
    if blocking:
        for f in blocking[:10]:
            vuln = f["vulnerability"]
            comp = f["component"]
            print(f"[{vuln['severity']}] {vuln['vulnId']}: {comp['name']}@{comp['version']}")
        sys.exit(1)
Pinning ML Dependencies and Lock Files

ML library stacks are notorious for silent breaking changes between minor versions (e.g., different default tokenizer behavior in transformers 4.40 vs 4.41). Pin every dependency to an exact version and commit the lock file. This makes SBOMs reproducible: the same commit hash always produces the same component list.

# Generate a fully-pinned requirements.txt from pyproject.toml
# Using pip-compile (pip-tools)
pip-compile pyproject.toml \
    --generate-hashes \        # adds SHA-256 hashes for each wheel — enables integrity check
    --output-file requirements.txt \
    --resolver=backtracking

# Install only from pinned file (never auto-upgrade in CI)
pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.txt

# pyproject.toml (direct dependencies only — let pip-compile resolve transitive)
[project]
dependencies = [
    "openai>=1.35,<2",
    "anthropic>=0.28,<1",
    "langchain>=0.2,<0.3",
    "fastapi>=0.111,<0.112",
    "pydantic>=2.7,<3",
]

[tool.pip-compile]
generate-hashes = true
reuse-hashes = true

Hash-pinned requirements mean pip will refuse to install a package whose downloaded wheel does not match the recorded SHA-256, preventing supply-chain attacks that swap a dependency's content without changing its version number.
Base Image Freshness and OS Package Updates

Python package CVEs get the most attention, but OS-level CVEs in the container base image are equally exploitable. Use a distroless or minimal base image and rebuild weekly — not just on code changes — so OS packages stay current. Record the base image digest in the SBOM so you can detect when a CVE affects the OS layer of a deployed image.

# Dockerfile — pin base image by digest for reproducibility
FROM python:3.11.9-slim-bookworm@sha256:da2d7af143dab7cd5b0d5a5c9545fe14e67fc24c394fcf1cf15e8ea16cbd8637

# Record base image metadata as OCI label (captured by syft)
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.base.name="python:3.11.9-slim-bookworm"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.base.digest="sha256:da2d7af143dab7cd5b0d5a5c9545fe14e67fc24c394fcf1cf15e8ea16cbd8637"

# Install only what's needed, then clean up to minimize OS package surface
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
        libgomp1 \       # required by some ML native extensions
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

COPY --chown=app:app requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r requirements.txt

COPY --chown=app:app src/ ./src/
USER app
CMD ["uvicorn", "src.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]

# .github/workflows/base-image-refresh.yml
# Weekly rebuild to pick up OS-level security updates
name: Weekly Base Image Refresh

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 2 * * 1'   # every Monday at 02:00 UTC
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  rebuild:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Pull latest base image and check for updates
        id: check
        run: |
          NEW_DIGEST=$(docker pull python:3.11-slim-bookworm --quiet | tail -1)
          CURRENT_DIGEST=$(grep 'FROM python' Dockerfile | grep -oP 'sha256:[a-f0-9]+')
          if [ "$NEW_DIGEST" != "$CURRENT_DIGEST" ]; then
            echo "update_needed=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          fi

      - name: Update Dockerfile digest and open PR
        if: steps.check.outputs.update_needed == 'true'
        run: |
          NEW_DIGEST=$(docker inspect python:3.11-slim-bookworm --format='{{index .RepoDigests 0}}' | cut -d@ -f2)
          sed -i "s|sha256:[a-f0-9]*|$NEW_DIGEST|g" Dockerfile
          git config user.email "ci@example.com"
          git config user.name "Base Image Bot"
          git checkout -b "chore/base-image-$(date +%Y%m%d)"
          git add Dockerfile
          git commit -m "chore: update python base image digest $(date +%Y-%m-%d)"
          git push origin HEAD
Summary
Run syft against the built container image (not the source directory alone) to capture both Python packages and OS-level packages in the base image.
Pipe the SBOM through grype and fail CI on HIGH or CRITICAL CVEs; maintain an expiring ignore list for accepted exceptions with documented rationale.
Attach the signed SBOM as a cosign OCI attestation so the inventory is cryptographically linked to a specific immutable image digest.
Archive SBOM JSON and vulnerability reports to S3 keyed by git commit SHA — required for post-incident forensics and regulatory audits.

GenAI services must record model versions (provider + model_id + version) in a companion model-manifest.json alongside the SBOM — LLM APIs are supply-chain components even though they are not Python packages.
Generate both SPDX 2.3 and CycloneDX 1.5 formats; they serve different downstream consumers and the dual-format cost at build time is under 30 seconds.
